county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1868 from Autauga, Bibb, Perry, and Shelby as Baker County; renamed Chilton 1874. County seat Clanton. Known as Alabama's peach-growing center. Louisville and Nashville Railroad commercial hub.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Clanton. Created in 1868 from parts of Autauga, Bibb, Perry, and Shelby — earlier records are in those parent counties. Originally named Baker County, renamed Chilton in 1874. The county is known as Alabama's peach capital; agricultural census records document farming families. The county had fewer large plantations than Black Belt counties, with more small and medium farms. Clanton's location on the Louisville and Nashville Railroad made it a commercial hub. The Chilton County Historical Society has local research materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books and marriage records. Check ADAH for early tax records from parent counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Chilton County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1847 from Sumter and Washington counties. County seat Butler. Tombigbee River valley county in the Choctaw Cession area. Named for the Choctaw Nation who ceded the land in the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Butler (the town, not Butler County). Choctaw County records begin in 1847, when it was formed from Sumter and Washington counties. The county lies in the Tombigbee River valley and was part of the Choctaw Cession after the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830). For Choctaw ancestry, check the Armstrong Roll and other removal records at NARA. The county was heavily forested; timber and lumber industry records may document families. The Choctaw County Historical Society has local materials. ADAH holds county tax records and some court records. FamilySearch has microfilmed deeds, probate, and marriages. For pre-1847 records, check Sumter and Washington counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Choctaw County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1812 from Washington County — one of Alabama's oldest counties. County seat Grove Hill. Site of the Fort Mims Massacre (1813) during the Creek War. Tombigbee River steamboat landing and trading center. Significant enslaved population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Grove Hill. Clarke County has records from 1812, making it one of Alabama's oldest counties with relatively good record survival. The county witnessed the Fort Mims Massacre (1813) during the Creek War — military records from this conflict are at NARA and ADAH. Clarke County's Tombigbee River location made it an important steamboat and trading center. The county had significant enslaved populations on river plantations. The Clarke County Historical Society in Grove Hill maintains local family files. Clarke County Democrat newspaper (from 1858) on microfilm at ADAH has genealogical notices. FamilySearch has microfilmed deed books, probate records, and marriages. Federal land patents via BLM GLO document original settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Clarke County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Randolph and Talladega counties. County seat Ashland. Appalachian foothill county with Cheaha Mountain, Alabama's highest point. Unionist sentiment during Civil War. Small farms predominated.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Ashland. Created in 1866 from Randolph and Talladega counties — pre-1866 records are in those parent counties. Clay County is in the Appalachian foothills with smaller farms and fewer enslaved persons than Black Belt counties. The county was a center of Unionist sentiment during the Civil War; some residents served in Union Alabama cavalry units. Cheaha Mountain (Alabama's highest point) is in Clay County. The county's economy was based on subsistence farming and small-scale mining. FamilySearch has microfilm for deeds and marriages. Check ADAH for military records on both Confederate and Union service. The Clay County GenWeb project has volunteer-compiled data.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Clay County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Calhoun, Randolph, and Talladega counties. County seat Heflin. Appalachian foothill county near Georgia line. Named for Confederate General Patrick Cleburne. Gold mining activity in 1840s-1850s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Heflin. Created in 1866 from Calhoun, Randolph, and Talladega — check parent counties for earlier records. Cleburne County is in the Appalachian foothills near the Georgia line. Like neighboring Clay County, it had Unionist leanings during the Civil War. The county was named for Confederate general Patrick Cleburne. Gold mining in the 1840s-1850s drew early settlers to the area. The Cleburne County Historical Society has local research files. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books and marriage records. The county's proximity to Georgia means many families had cross-border connections — check Randolph County, Georgia records as well.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Cleburne County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1866 courthouse fire destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1841 from Dale County. County seat Elba. 1866 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. Pea River frequently flooded Elba. Enterprise is known for the Boll Weevil Monument (1919) celebrating diversification from cotton to peanuts.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Elba. The 1866 courthouse fire destroyed most early records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch microfilm for surviving copies and state-level duplicates. Coffee County was formed from Dale County in 1841. The Pea River regularly flooded Elba, causing additional record damage. Enterprise (in the western part of the county) became a major town after the boll weevil forced diversification from cotton to peanuts — the Boll Weevil Monument (1919) commemorates this. The Coffee County Historical Society has local records. FamilySearch microfilm covers surviving deeds and marriages. For pre-1841 records, check Dale County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Coffee County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1867 from Franklin County. County seat Tuscumbia. Tennessee Valley county in the Muscle Shoals area. Helen Keller's birthplace (Ivy Green) in Tuscumbia. Wilson Dam and TVA transformed the region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Tuscumbia. Created in 1867 from Franklin County. Colbert County is in the Muscle Shoals area of the Tennessee River valley. Helen Keller's birthplace (Ivy Green) is in Tuscumbia. The Muscle Shoals area attracted early settlers due to fertile land. Wilson Dam and the TVA transformed the area in the 1920s-1930s. The Colbert County Historical Landmarks Foundation has local materials. Sheffield and Muscle Shoals city records supplement county records. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages. For pre-1867 records, check Franklin County. ADAH holds county tax records. The Tennessee Valley Genealogical Society covers this region.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Colbert County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1866 fire destroyed courthouse and most records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Monroe County — one of Alabama's earliest counties. County seat Evergreen. 1866 fire destroyed courthouse and most records. Economy based on cotton, timber, and naval stores (turpentine). Conecuh River valley settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Evergreen. The 1866 fire destroyed the courthouse and most records — this is a burned county. For pre-1866 records, check ADAH microfilm, FamilySearch, and state-level copies. Conecuh County was formed from Monroe County in 1818 and was one of Alabama's earliest counties. The county's economy was based on cotton, timber, and naval stores (turpentine, rosin). The Conecuh River shaped settlement patterns. The county had significant enslaved populations on cotton plantations. The Evergreen-Conecuh County Public Library has local history materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records from the 1866 restart.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Conecuh County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1866 fire destroyed courthouse; early records lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat Rockford. 1866 fire destroyed courthouse and early records. Gold mining at Goldville (Hog Mountain) in 1840s. Creek resistance before removal occurred in this area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Rockford. The 1866 fire destroyed the courthouse and early records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for surviving copies. Coosa County was formed from Creek Cession lands in 1832. The county saw the final Creek resistance before removal; some mixed-heritage Creek families remained. Coosa County had significant gold mining activity in the 1840s at Hog Mountain (Goldville). The county's small population and rural character mean fewer supplementary records exist. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving deeds and marriages. Federal land patents via BLM GLO document original white settlers who took Creek lands.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Coosa County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, inventories, and administrations for Coosa County; Probate Judge handles wills and estates in Alabama; ADAH has microfilm of historical probate records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Burned county: 1866 fire destroyed courthouse; early records lost

source_profiles.source_name: Coosa County Marriage Records -- Probate Judge / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Coosa County Probate Judge, Rockford / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and bonds for Coosa County from 1866; Probate Judge issues marriage licenses in Alabama; FamilySearch has indexed many Alabama marriages

source_profiles.source_name: Coosa County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Coosa County; Alabama first enumerated in 1820 federal census; slave schedules in 1850 and 1860; agricultural and manufacturing schedules available

source_profiles.source_name: Coosa County Cherokee/Creek Removal Records -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cherokee and Creek removal records related to Coosa County including census rolls (Henderson Roll 1835), emigration records, claims files, and treaty documents; essential for tracing Native American families who lived in the area before removal

source_profiles.source_name: Coosa County Federal Land Patents -- BLM General Land Office
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Coosa County from the General Land Office; Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land from the federal government; patents searchable and downloadable at BLM GLO website

source_profiles.source_name: Coosa County Historic Newspapers -- ADAH / Chronicling America
source_profiles.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers serving Coosa County; legal notices, obituaries, marriage announcements, estate settlements; ADAH has microfilmed Alabama newspapers; Chronicling America has digitized titles

county_metadata.record_losses: 1895 fire destroyed courthouse and many records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1821 from Henry County. County seat Andalusia (since 1899). 1895 fire destroyed courthouse and many records. Timber, turpentine, and peanut economy. Three Notch Road (Federal Road) was the main early route.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Andalusia. The 1895 fire destroyed the courthouse and many records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH for microfilm copies and state-level duplicates. Covington County was formed in 1821 from Henry County. The county's economy was dominated by timber, turpentine, and later peanut farming. Andalusia became the county seat in 1899 after the fire. The Covington Historical Society maintains local family files and cemetery records. The Three Notch Museum in Andalusia documents the Federal Road (Three Notch Road) that was the main early route through the county. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records from the post-1895 restart.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Covington County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.repository: ADAH / FamilySearch / Local churches and cemeteries
